---
title: "Amsterdam Airbnb Stay Value Predictor"
subtitle: "Can we predict price and booking demand from listing features?"
author: "Manca Levašič"
date: "June 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    logo: ""
    footer: "MADA Capstone · Amsterdam Airbnb · 2026"
    chalkboard: false
    highlight-style: github
---

## The Problem {background-color="#f0f4ff"}

I am planning a trip to Amsterdam and I wanted to answer two questions every traveller has:

::: {.incremental}
- 💰 **Is this listing fairly priced?** *(regression)*
- 📅 **Will it actually be available when I want to book?** *(classification)*
:::

. . .

**Dataset:** Inside Airbnb Amsterdam — 10,480 listings, 79 columns, scraped September 2025.

Public data · Creative Commons · No personal information.

---

## What We Built {background-color="#f0fff4"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Offline (once)
1. Download raw CSV
2. Clean + engineer features
3. Train two XGBoost models
4. Save as `.pkl` files
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Live App (always on)
1. Streamlit loads both models at startup
2. User fills in listing details
3. Models return: **price estimate + availability label**
:::
:::

. . .

> One pipeline. Two questions. One app.

---

## The Data

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**Raw → Clean:**

| Step | Rows |
|------|------|
| Raw dataset | 10,480 |
| Remove missing/zero price | −4,665 |
| Cap outliers (>€992 = 99th %ile) | −few |
| **Final** | **5,815** |

**27 features** used for modelling:
23 numeric · 4 categorical
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**Key engineered features:**

- `host_years` — from `host_since` date
- `amenity_count` — length of amenities list
- `review_composite` — mean of 6 review dimensions
- `beds_per_person` — ratio
- `multi_listing_host` — boolean flag
:::
:::

---

## No Data Leakage {background-color="#fff8e1"}

::: {.callout-warning}
## The #1 mistake in ML projects
Fitting the scaler or encoder on *all* data before splitting inflates your score.
You end up with a model that looks great but fails in production.
:::

. . .

**How we avoided it:**

- All preprocessing inside `sklearn Pipeline` — fitted on train data only
- `availability_30/60/90/365` columns **dropped** from the feature set (they *are* the target)
- Scalers/encoders re-fit inside each CV fold via `GridSearchCV`
- Test set touched **exactly once** at the very end

---

## Model Ladder — Both Tasks

We train 4 models per task, from simple to complex:

| Step | Classification | Regression |
|------|---------------|------------|
| **Baseline** | DummyClassifier | DummyRegressor |
| **Linear** | Logistic Regression | Ridge Regression |
| **Tree** | Decision Tree | Decision Tree |
| **XGBoost** | XGBClassifier ✅ | XGBRegressor ✅ |

. . .

> Always start with the baseline. A model that cannot beat Dummy is not done.

---

## Overfitting Demo

A Decision Tree with **unlimited depth** memorises the training set:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Classification:**

| | Train AUC | Val AUC |
|---|---|---|
| DT (unlimited) | **1.000** | 0.604 |
| DT (depth = 5) | 0.68 | 0.636 |
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Regression:**

| | Train R² | Val R² |
|---|---|---|
| DT (unlimited) | **1.000** | 0.165 |
| DT (depth = 5) | 0.53 | 0.438 |
:::
:::

. . .

> Perfect on training data ≠ useful model. XGBoost controls this via regularisation (subsample, colsample, learning rate).

---

## XGBoost Tuning

5-fold **cross-validation** + `GridSearchCV` over:

```
max_depth:     [3, 4]
learning_rate: [0.05, 0.1]
n_estimators:  [200, 300]
```

. . .

**Best params found:**

| Task | max_depth | lr | n_estimators |
|------|-----------|-----|--------------|
| Classification | 4 | 0.05 | 300 |
| Regression | 4 | 0.10 | 200 |

> Cross-validation gives a stable estimate of performance — averaged over 5 folds — instead of relying on one lucky split.

---

## Results — Classification {background-color="#fef2f2"}

**Can we predict which listings have ≤ 90 free days/year?**

| Model | Accuracy | F1 | **ROC-AUC** |
|-------|----------|-----|------------|
| Dummy (baseline) | 0.562 | 0.000 | 0.500 |
| Logistic Regression | 0.623 | 0.536 | 0.655 |
| Decision Tree (d=5) | 0.616 | 0.487 | 0.636 |
| **XGBoost** | **0.647** | **0.565** | **0.698** |

. . .

> **AUC 0.70** — meaningfully better than random. The model captures real signal, but `low_availability` is a noisy proxy: hosts can block dates manually.

---

## Results — Regression {background-color="#f0fff4"}

**Can we predict the nightly price?**

| Model | **R²** | MAE | RMSE |
|-------|--------|-----|------|
| Dummy (baseline) | 0.000 | €100 | €147 |
| Ridge Regression | 0.580 | €68 | €106 |
| Decision Tree (d=5) | 0.438 | €77 | €115 |
| **XGBoost** | **0.627** | **€63** | **€97** |

. . .

> **R² = 0.63, MAE = €63.** XGBoost explains 63% of price variance and is on average €63 off — useful for spotting under- or over-priced listings.

---

## What Drives Price?

**Top predictors (XGBoost feature importance):**

1. **Room type** — entire home vs private room is the biggest price driver
2. **Accommodates** — more guests → higher price
3. **Neighbourhood** — Centrum-West commands a premium
4. **Amenity count** — well-equipped listings charge more
5. **Review scores** — higher rated = higher priced

. . .

> These align with intuition — which is a good sign the model isn't learning noise.

---

## Limitations — Be Honest {background-color="#fff8e1"}

::: {.callout-important}
A model that cannot explain its failures is not trustworthy.
:::

- `low_availability` is a proxy, not pure demand — hosts can block dates
- Prices above ~€300/night have sparse training data → uncertainty grows
- One snapshot in time — no seasonal adjustment
- Correlation ≠ causation: improving reviews does not automatically raise price
- R² = 0.63 means **37% of price variation is unexplained** — useful, not magic

---

## The Live App

A **Streamlit** web app where any user can:

1. Fill in listing characteristics in a sidebar form
2. Click **Predict**
3. See: estimated nightly price + availability prediction + confidence bar

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Deployed at:**
`share.streamlit.io` (link in README)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Stack:**
Python · scikit-learn · XGBoost · Streamlit · joblib
:::
:::

---

## Key Takeaways {background-color="#f0f4ff"}

::: {.incremental}
1. **Beat the baseline** — XGBoost beats DummyRegressor/DummyClassifier on both tasks ✅
2. **No leakage** — all preprocessing inside Pipeline, test set untouched until the end ✅
3. **Honest evaluation** — report limitations, do not oversell ✅
4. **Deployed** — a real app anyone can use to compare Amsterdam listings ✅
:::

. . .

> **The most useful insight:** Room type and neighbourhood explain most of the price — the same tools that data scientists use every day, applied to a dataset I actually care about.

---

## Thank You {background-color="#f0fff4"}

**Manca Levašič · MADA Capstone 2026**

Dataset: Inside Airbnb Amsterdam (CC-BY 4.0)

GitHub repo · Live app · Full Quarto report

. . .

*Questions?*
